insights: Finance department 'Cost Reduction' goals experienced a 92% increase in mean duration from Q1 (184 days) to Q4 (354 days) in 2022, reflecting a systematic, linear escalation rather than random variation (R² = 1.0).

Owner assignment is the dominant driver of duration variance for these goals, explaining 60.4% of total variation—far exceeding all other factors including priority, state, and completion metrics, which collectively explain less than 5%.

The Q1→Q4 duration increase is driven by two interlocking mechanisms: (1) compositional shift—slower-performing owners received disproportionately more assignments in later quarters (61.7% of the 170-day increase), and (2) universal within-owner degradation—all 25 owners with multi-quarter data showed worsening performance over time (38.3% of the increase).

The near-perfect within-owner time-duration correlation (mean r = 0.9999) and zero improving owners confirm that the duration escalation is systemic—not attributable to individual underperformance or isolated incidents, but to organization-wide process or resource pressures.

Expense Ratio–type Cost Reduction goals have the highest mean duration (295 days), suggesting metric-specific complexity or governance overhead, though this factor explains only 11.6% of variance and does not override the primacy of owner-level dynamics.

With only 50 of 108 Finance Cost Reduction goals completed—and 126 remaining 'In Progress'—the escalating durations correlate with a growing operational backlog, indicating mounting service pressure and potential risk to annual cost reduction targets.
summary: The core business issue is a systematic, quarterly-escalating delay in Finance department 'Cost Reduction' goal execution—duration nearly doubled from 184 to 354 days in 2022—driven overwhelmingly by how ownership is assigned and managed over time. Owner assignment explains over 60% of duration variance, and the escalation breaks down into two equally consequential components: slower owners absorbing more new goals in later quarters (61.7%), and every owner’s performance degrading across time (38.3%). This is not an individual accountability issue but a structural one—evidenced by perfect within-owner temporal correlations and zero improvement cases. The high 'In Progress' rate (126/108) and low completion count (50) further signal accumulating execution risk. Next, focus must shift to diagnosing root causes behind the universal owner degradation—such as workload imbalance, insufficient support tools, unclear success criteria, or cascading dependencies—and redesigning the goal assignment and monitoring framework to decouple duration from calendar time.
